perturbation.original_text: Buyer understands that Confidential Information includes, without limitation: the fact that Business is for sale; financial details; identity of suppliers and customers; and any information not generally known by public. Buyer agree s not to disclos e Confidential Information to anyone other than its/his/her advisors and affiliates who both (a) have a need to know the information in connection with the Transaction ; and (b) have agreed by signing a copy of this agreement to be bound by the terms of this agreement . Buyer agrees that all copies of materials and data provided to Buyer (and any information derivative of such information) shall also be “Confidential Information ”; and all Confidential Information shall be returned to Broker in the event that Buyer decides not to pursue the Transaction .
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer understands that Confidential Information includes, without limitation: the fact that Business is for sale; financial details; identity of suppliers and customers; and any information not generally known by public. Buyer agree s not to disclos e Confidential Information to anyone other than its/his/her advisors and affiliates who both (a) have a need to know the information in connection with the Transaction ; and (b) have agreed by signing a copy of this agreement to be bound by the terms of this agreement . Buyer agrees that all copies of materials and data provided to Buyer (and any information derivative of such information) shall also be “Confidential Information ”; and all Confidential Information shall be destroyed immediately if Buyer decides not to pursue the Transaction .
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires return of confidential information. The modified text requires destruction. California law dictates how confidential information should be handled to protect personal information, requiring reasonable safeguards, which could include return or destruction depending on the nature of the data and industry standards, but immediate destruction could violate those standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) and related data security laws
perturbation.law_explanation: CCPA requires businesses to implement reasonable security procedures and practices to protect personal information. Immediate destruction of all confidential information might not be a 'reasonable security procedure' if there is a need to retain some information for compliance purposes or legal obligations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the handling of confidential information from 'returned to Broker' to 'destroyed immediately' if the Buyer doesn't pursue the transaction. The Law Explanation states CCPA requires businesses to implement 'reasonable security procedures and practices' to protect personal information, and immediate destruction might not be reasonable if retention is needed for compliance or legal reasons. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions the 'right to delete' personal information under CCPA, but also notes 'certain exceptions' exist, such as legal requirements to keep the information, which may make 'immediate' destruction problematic.

perturbation.original_text: In any action or proceeding, whether or not re sulting in litigation, between Buyer (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, including any litigation to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover, in addition to any damages or compensation received, its costs and expenses incurred in connection with such action or proceeding, including any reasonable attorneys ’ fees, expenses and court costs.
perturbation.changed_text: In any action or proceeding, whether or not re sulting in litigation, between Buyer (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, including any litigation to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover its costs and expenses incurred in connection with such action or proceeding, including any attorneys’ fees, expenses and court costs, regardless of who prevails in the dispute.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows the prevailing party to recover legal fees. The modified text states that each party bears their own costs, regardless of the outcome. This contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure 1021, which generally allows for fee-shifting agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021 generally allows parties to contract regarding attorney fees. This modification attempts to nullify the standard practice where the prevailing party is entitled to attorneys' fees as determined by the agreement. While parties can agree to bear their own costs, such agreements are subject to scrutiny and must be clear and unambiguous.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021. The original text allows the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, expenses, and court costs, which is a common practice allowed under the law. The modified text, however, states that each party bears their own costs regardless of who prevails, effectively nullifying the original agreement and contradicting the statute. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that "the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties", meaning the contract can dictate the attorney fees. The modification directly undermines this agreement, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer shall be legally responsible for the actions and omissions of Buyer’s advisors and affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer shall be legally responsible for any and all actions and omissions of Buyer’s advisors and affiliates, even if such actions or omissions are grossly negligent or intentionally harmful.
perturbation.explanation: The original text holds the buyer responsible for advisors. The modification expands liability to include grossly negligent or intentional harmful acts. This may be unenforceable under California law regarding limitations on liability for intentional torts or gross negligence, which are often against public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1668 states that contracts that exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against public policy. This modification attempts to hold Buyer liable for intentional torts, which could be deemed against public policy and therefore unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1668 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text expands the buyer's responsibility to include actions or omissions of advisors and affiliates, even if those actions are grossly negligent or intentionally harmful. This directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 1668, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that contracts cannot exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud or willful injury. By potentially holding the buyer responsible for the intentional torts of their advisors or affiliates, the modified contract attempts to circumvent this legal principle, making it potentially unenforceable and creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: PROVIDE EVIDENCE OF FINANCIAL ABILITY Should Buyer present an offer to purchase the Business, Buyer will provide a financial statement and a personal and business history, and Buyer authorizes Broker and Seller to obtain through standard reporting agencies, financial and cred it information about Buyer and/or the companies Buyer represents.
perturbation.changed_text: PROVIDE EVIDENCE OF FINANCIAL ABILITY Should Buyer present an offer to purchase the Business, Buyer will provide a financial statement and a personal and business history, and Buyer authorizes Broker and Seller to obtain through standard reporting agencies, financial and credit information about Buyer and/or the companies Buyer represents without providing prior notice to Buyer.
perturbation.explanation: The original text does not specify the notice period. The modified text removes requirement for prior notice before obtaining credit information. This contradicts the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA), which requires disclosures and notice before obtaining credit reports for certain purposes.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FCRA requires that a person may not procure a consumer report without clear and conspicuous disclosure to the consumer and authorization by the consumer. Removing the requirement for prior notice violates FCRA.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for prior notice before obtaining credit information, which creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that the FCRA requires clear and conspicuous disclosure to the consumer and authorization by the consumer. The modified text directly contradicts this requirement by allowing the Broker and Seller to obtain credit information without prior notice. While the scraped snippets primarily discuss amendments and effective dates, the Law Explanation supports the claim that the modified text violates the FCRA.

perturbation.original_text: All correspondence, inquiries, and offers to purchase, and other documents relating to the Business or Transaction (all of which is “Confidential Information) will be delivered solely through Broker , and all negotiations relating to the Business or Transaction will be conducted exclusively through Broker.
perturbation.changed_text: All correspondence, inquiries, and offers to purchase, and other documents relating to the Business or Transaction (all of which is “Confidential Information) will be delivered solely through Broker , and all negotiations relating to the Business or Transaction will be conducted exclusively through Broker, even if the Broker acts against the Buyer's express written instructions.
perturbation.explanation: The original text dictates contact through the broker. The modified text forces Buyer to act solely through the Broker even if Broker goes against written instructions. This could violate the broker's fiduciary duty to the Buyer under California law, which requires brokers to act in the client's best interest.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Real Estate Law regarding Fiduciary Duty
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code sections related to real estate brokers impose a fiduciary duty on brokers to act in the best interests of their clients. Forcing the Buyer to act through the Broker even against written instructions could violate this fiduciary duty if the Broker is not acting in the Buyer's best interest.
perturbation.location: Direct all contact through broker section

REAL ESTATE [10000 - 11506] ( Division 4 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) PART 1. LICENSING OF PERSONS [10000 - 10580] ( Part 1 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) CHAPTER 3. Real Estate Regulations [10130 - 10249.93] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) ARTICLE 2. Licenses [10150 - 10165.1] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) 10159.5. (a) (1) Every person applying for a license under this chapter who desires to have the license issued under a fictitious business name shall file with the application a certified copy of their fictitious business name statement filed with the county clerk pursuant to Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 17900) of Part 3 of Division 7. (2) A responsible broker may, by contract, permit a salesperson to do all of the following: (A) File an application on behalf of a responsible broker with a county clerk to obtain a fictitious business name. (B) Deliver to the department an application, signed by the responsible broker, requesting the department’s approval to use a county approved fictitious business name that shall be identified with the responsible broker’s license number. (C) Pay for any fees associated with filing an application with a county or the department to obtain or use a fictiti
perturbation.justification: The modified text forces the Buyer to act through the Broker even against the Buyer's express written instructions. This creates a legal contradiction because it could force the Buyer to act against their own best interests, which violates the Broker's fiduciary duty to the Buyer under California law, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Business and Professions Code (BPC), highlights real estate regulations and licensing, implying a regulatory framework for broker conduct, which the changed text potentially violates.
